segments.s101-g1: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' marking origin. ترکان طلایه بسی is a noun phrase with ترکان (Turks) as the source and طلایه (vanguard) as the head, modified by بسی (an intensive quantifier placed after طلایه in classical word order). بد براه is an adverbial phrase attached to the coming verb in the next segment meaning 'in bad plight / ill-omened on the road' (بد modifies براه).
segments.s101-g2: رسید is a 3rd-person singular past 'arrived/reached'. اندر ایشان is a locative phrase 'among them/within them' (اندر before the pronoun). یل صف پناه is a nominal apposition: یل 'a hero' is qualified by the compound صف پناه 'refuge/protector of the line' (a genitive/attributive relationship expressed as a noun phrase). The whole segment says that such a hero arrived into their midst.
segments.s101-g3: برآویخت is a past tense verb 'sprang up/rose up' used here to indicate beginning of the attack. با introduces the company/means 'with' and نامداران جنگ is a classical nominal phrase 'the renowned ones of battle' (نامداران modified by جنگ, i.e. famous warriors). The clause therefore means 'he sprang up together with the famed warriors for battle.'
segments.s101-g4: یکی is an indefinite 'one / someone' introducing a single fighter. گرزه is the direct object 'mace' and گاو پیکر is a compound adjective after the noun meaning 'bull-bodied' (a stocky, powerful build). به چنگ is a frozen locution meaning 'in (his) grasp / in hand' where به marks the instrument/place and چنگ denotes the hold/grip. The segment describes one fierce man seizing a mace.
segments.s101-g5: دلیران توران is the subject 'the brave ones of Turan' (noun + geographic identifier). برآویختند is the 3rd-person plural past 'they sprang upon/rose up,' matching the plural subject; the verb conveys sudden aggressive action by those Turanian warriors.
segments.s101-g6: سرانجام is an adverb 'finally / at last' placed before the verbal phrase. از رزم is the prepositional phrase 'from the battle' (از marking separation or source). بگریختند is the 3rd-person plural past 'they fled / escaped.' Together: 'in the end they fled from the battle.'
segments.s101-g7: نهادند is the past-tense verb 'they placed / they set' used idiomatically with سر سوی to mean 'they turned their heads toward.' سر (head) is the object placed before the directional complement سوی (toward), and افراسیاب is the goal/name. This is a common classical phrasing where 'placing the head toward X' = 'looked/turned to X.'
segments.s101-g8: همه quantifies the people ('all/everyone'). The pattern X پر از Y (دل پر از خون) is a stative expression: دل 'heart' + پر 'full' + از خون 'of blood' (meaning wounded or full of grief) and دیده پر آب likewise 'eye full of water' (i.e. eyes full of tears). The clause describes the emotional/physical state of everyone mentioned.
segments.s101-g65: Literal word order is agent-phrase + verb: 'به دست دگر' (by/with the other hand) is a prepositional-like phrase placing the instrument before the verb. جام پر باده کرد is a simple past transitive clause: جام (cup) + پر (full/of) + باده (wine) + کرد (did/made) — here کرد is the past tense 'he filled/made' (poetic past of کردن used with a noun phrase to mean 'to fill/make X').
segments.s101-g66: وزو is a fused old/poetic form meaning 'and from it / thereby' (here functioning adverbially) — the provided dictionary sense doesn't match, so no provided sense fits exactly. یاد مردان آزاده کرد is another past transitive clause: یاد (remembrance/commemoration) + مردان آزاده (the noble/free men, in genitive apposition) + کرد (he made/did) — thus 'he commemorated / recalled the noble men.'
segments.s101-g67: This clause uses object first then verb: 'دگر جام' (the other cup) + 'بر دست رستم' (into/onto Rostam's hand; بر here has the directional/onto/into sense) + 'سپرد' (past of سپردن, 'entrusted/handed over'). The verb سپرد is simple past, showing he gave/entrusted the cup to Rostam.
segments.s101-g68: بدو گفت is the short inverted phrase 'to him said' = 'he said to him' (bedo = 'to him/unto it' precedes the speech verb). گفت here is the narrative past 'said' (the provided sense list gives noun senses, so none fit exactly). کای is a vocative 'O', and the following نامبردار و گرد are vocative attributes: نامبردار = 'renowned/famed' and گرد appears as a poetic epithet (here functioning as part of the vocative phrase).
segments.s101-g69: بپرسیدی is a past/interrogative/conditional second-person form meaning roughly 'had you asked' or 'if you had asked' (a classical/poetic past rendering of a counterfactual). از من = 'of/from me' marks the source of the information, and نشان قباد is a genitive phrase 'Qobad's sign/token.' The clause as a whole functions as the protasis ('had you asked me about Qobad's token...').
segments.s101-g70: This is the tagged question: تو (you) + این نام را (this name, را marks it as definite object/topic) + از که داری (from whom do you have/hold) + به یاد (in memory). داری is present/past-marked present-tense sense 'you have/hold' here used to ask whose memory preserves the name. Word order places the object before the question phrase for emphasis.
segments.s101-g71: بدو گفت رستم is another inverted phrase: 'to him said Rostam' = 'Rostam said to him.' که introduces a subordinate clause ('that'); here که + از پهلوان introduces the content of the message: 'that concerning the champion' (از functioning as 'about/of'). گفت as printed carries only noun senses in the provided list, so no listed sense matches the verbal past 'said.'
segments.s101-g72: پیام آوریدم is a past first-person compound: پیام (message) + آوریدم (I brought/ I brought forth) — a straightforward past tense 'I brought a message.' به روشن‌روان is the indirect object/recipient phrase 'to the bright/fair‑souled one.' The clause is a reported statement of having brought tidings.
segments.s101-g33: This is a nominal/clausal group describing the action as a single rising: رده (a row/line) is modified by the past-participle برکشیده 'raised/erected', and بسی پهلوان supplies the plurality/agents (many champions). Word order is poetic/inverted: the idea is 'many champions made a raised row' = 'many champions rose up in a line.'
segments.s101-g34: به رسم بزرگان is a prepositional phrase 'in the custom/manner of the great ones (elders)'. کمر بر میان is a compact verbal phrase meaning 'gird (the) waist about the middle' where the verb 'to gird' (بستن) is omitted by ellipsis; thus the phrase functions as 'girded their loins (around the middle)'.
segments.s101-g35: A noun phrase in which بیاراسته (literally 'unadorned' in the dictionary entry) qualifies مجلسی 'of the assembly/stately', and شاهوار ('regal/majestic') further characterizes the assembly. The adjectives precede the head noun in a compact, poetic stacking.
segments.s101-g36: بسان introduces a simile 'like/as', followed by بهشتی 'heavenly/paradisal'. به رنگ و نگار is a prepositional phrase 'in colour and ornament/decoration' modifying the comparison; here به means 'in' and نگار is used in the sense of painting/ornamentation.
segments.s101-g37: چو 'when' introduces a temporal clause; دیدند is the 3rd-person plural past 'they saw'. پهلوان را is the definite object 'the champion'. به راه is an adverbial phrase 'on/along the road' modifying دیدند. The short pronoun مر is archaic/poetic and ambiguous in isolation (see senses); here it appears as an enclitic/pronoun-like element tied into the clause and does not change the basic 'they saw the champion on the road' meaning.
segments.s101-g38: پذیره is used adjectivally/nominally 'receptive/receiving' and combines with شدند (they became) plus the enclitic -ش (him) to yield the notion 'they received him' (poetic alternative to modern پذیرفتندش). ازان سایه‌گاه 'from that shady place' specifies the origin/place from which they brought or welcomed him.
segments.s101-g39: که introduces the explanatory clause 'for' or 'that', ما میزبانیم is a present declarative 'we are hosts', and و مهمان ما places 'guest' with our possessive (marginal vocative/identification: 'and you are our guest' understood). The clause states hospitality before the invitation.
segments.s101-g40: فرود آی is an imperative cluster meaning 'come down' (فرود = descent/coming down, آی = 'come!' imperative). ایذر 'here' is an adverb of place, and به فرمان ما 'at/by our command' uses به in the sense 'by/in accordance with' plus فرمان 'command' and ما 'our'. The whole is a direct invitation/order: 'Come down here at our command.'
segments.s101-g57: بگوییم is an optative/imperative-like first-person-plural verb meaning 'let us say' (here introducing a narration); یکسر is an adverb modifying the verb meaning 'at once' or 'straightaway'. نشان (literally 'sign/mark') is the object being announced, and قباد is the name in apposition. Word order is Verb + adverb + object + name (typical poetic order).
segments.s101-g58: که introduces a relative/subordinate clause 'which/that' (not causal here). او را uses the personal pronoun with the object marker را to mark the theme/emphasized referent. چگونست is an archaic/poetic contraction of 'چگونه است' meaning 'how is it' or 'what ... was'; رسم و نهاد is a compound of 'manner/custom' + 'disposition/nature', the second noun linked by و. The clause thus asks 'what his manners and nature were.'
segments.s101-g59: The segment begins with the name/epithet تهمتن (the mighty one) as subject, followed by the prepositional phrase ز رخش ('from his steed') and the past verb اندر آمد ('came down' / 'alighted'). The simile چو باد ('like the wind') follows to qualify the manner (i.e. he descended swiftly). This is a compact poetic word order: Subject + source + verb + simile.
segments.s101-g60: چو here = 'when' introducing the following temporal clause. بشنید is the simple past 'he heard'. از + وی means 'of/from him' or 'concerning him' (i.e. 'he heard about him'), followed by نشان قباد 'Qobad's sign/mark' as the content heard. The clause modifies the previous action: he descended when he heard of that sign.
segments.s101-g61: بیامد is a past-tense verb 'came'. دمان here (poetic) denotes the edge/skirts (contextually the moving party or their skirts/edge) and is followed by تا 'as far as' + لب رودبار 'the river's bank' (لب = 'brink/edge', رودبار = 'river/stream area'). The phrase yields 'they/it came as far as the riverbank.'
segments.s101-g62: نشستند is the past plural 'they sat down' (verb + 3rd-plural ending). در زیر is a prepositional phrase 'in/under', followed by آن سایه‌دار 'that shaded place' (سایه‌دار functioning as adjective/noun). Together: 'they sat beneath that shade.'
segments.s101-g63: جوان is the subject 'a youth/young man.' از بر تخت خود برنشست is a verbal phrase where برنشست (past) means 'rose up' or 'stood up' and از بر تخت خود literally 'from upon his throne/seat' indicates the place he rose from. Note the poetic order: Subject + source phrase + verb.
segments.s101-g64: گرفته is a perfective/participial form 'having seized' or simply 'seized/took'. یکی دست رستم = 'one hand of Rostam' or 'Rostam with one hand'; the following به دست indicates the manner/target ('by the hand' / 'by the hand of'). The whole line is best read as 'Rostam took him by the hand' (Rostam grasping the youth's hand). Word order is compact and elliptic, typical of epic verse.
segments.s101-g25: یکی is the indefinite numeral used to list or single out 'one' item. میل here is a physical pillar/milestone (poetic usage) followed by ره 'road/way' to form 'mile/marker of the road' (noun + noun). تا به ... کوه is a prepositional phrase 'as far as / up to Mount Alborz' with تا 'as far as' and به 'to/towards' introducing the proper name البرز followed by the generic کوه 'mountain' (name + generic). The whole segment is a nominal phrase (not a full clause) enumerating something seen.
segments.s101-g26: یکی again marks 'one' or 'a certain' item. جایگه is 'place/seat' (a locality or spot). دید is the past-tense verb 'he saw' (3rd singular) and governs the following apposition. برنا is an adjectival/epithet 'young' modifying شکوه; شکوه is a noun 'splendour' — together 'a young splendour' idiomatically meaning 'a splendid young man.' Word order reflects poetic inversion: object/apposition follows the verb.
segments.s101-g27: درختان بسیاری is 'many trees' (درختان plural 'trees' with بسیار as quantifier 'many'). و links the two coordinated elements. آب روان is a noun + postposed adjective 'water flowing' (روان 'flowing' describes آب). This is a descriptive nominal phrase listing features of the place.
segments.s101-g28: نشستنگه is a compound noun 'seat / place for sitting' (a locus of sitting). مردم نوجوان is a genitive/attributive phrase 'the people / folk — young' i.e. 'young people.' Together the phrase means 'a sitting-place of young people' or 'where the young folk sit.'
segments.s101-g29: یکی marks 'one / a certain' again. تخت بنهاده is a noun + past participle: تخت 'couch/throne' with بنهاده (from بنهادن) 'set down/placed' — together 'a couch that had been placed.' نزدیک آب is a locative phrase 'near the water' modifying where the couch stood. The whole segment is a nominal-phrase description.
segments.s101-g30: برو here is an archaic/poetic contraction of بر او / بر آن 'upon it' rather than the modern verb 'go' — it introduces what is on the couch. ریخته is the past participle 'poured/scattered' describing that pure musk (مشک ناب) and rosewater (گلاب) had been strewn or sprinkled there. The sequence places the scented substances on the couch.
segments.s101-g31: جوانی is 'a youth / young man.' به کردار is an idiomatic prepositional phrase 'in the manner/guise of' (کردار here conveys 'manner/appearance'). تابنده is an adjective 'shining/radiant' modifying ماه 'the moon,' a common Persian simile for beauty. The phrase describes the youth's appearance as like the radiant moon.
segments.s101-g32: نشسته is the past participle used predicatively 'seated.' بران is the demonstrative contraction 'بر آن' = 'on/upon that' (referring back to the couch). تخت is 'couch/throne.' بر سایه‌گاه is a locative phrase 'in/on the shady place' (بر here as a preposition meaning 'in/at' + سایه‌گاه 'shade-place'). Together: 'seated on that couch in the shade.'
segments.s101-g17: که introduces a relative clause ('who/that') describing ایرانیان. ایرانیان is the plural subject 'Iranians'. مردمی is an attributive/nominal predicate meaning 'a people' (here functioning like an apposition: 'Iranians, a people ...'). ریمنند is a present/habitual third‑person plural verb (root + -ند) meaning 'they rush' — the copula is omitted, so the clause reads 'who are a people who rush'.
segments.s101-g18: همی is an archaic/epic adverbial particle here giving emphasis (an emphatic/iterative sense). ناگهان is the adverb 'suddenly'. بر is used here with زدن to mark the target/recipient of the action ('against/upon'). طلایه is the object 'vanguard'. زن + ند actually forms the verb زَند ('they strike/attack'); the segment splits stem and plural ending, so زن = 'strike' and ند is the plural verbal ending (not a lexical negative).
segments.s101-g19: برون آمد is a compound: برون 'out/forth' + آمد 'came' (past tense). از نزد takes the source/location meaning 'from the presence of'. خسرو قلون is a proper name (Khosrow Qalun) or a titulary phrase; نزد + name = 'in the presence of Khosrow'.
segments.s101-g20: به marks direction/goal ('toward/into'). پیش here means 'before' or 'in front of' and with اندرون (the inner part/inner quarters) gives the destination 'to the front of the inner court'. مردم is the direct object 'the people' and رهنمون is a noun meaning 'guide/guidance' used predicatively: together they mean 'he led/was guide to the people' (i.e. he guided the people into the inner quarters). Word order is directional phrase (به پیش اندرون) + object (مردم) + role (رهنمون).
segments.s101-g21: سر راه is an adverbial phrase meaning 'on the road' or 'at the roadside' (سر + راه). بر is used here as a preposition meaning 'against/opposite' and takes نامداران ('the notable/renowned men') as its object. ببست is the past tense (3rd person singular) of بستن used in the sense 'he closed/blocked/placed a blockade' — thus: 'he took up position on the road against the notables.'
segments.s101-g22: به here marks accompaniment or instrument ('with' / 'by means of'). مردان جنگی is a compound 'warlike men' = 'warriors'. و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. پیلان is the plural of پیل 'elephant'. مست is an adjective following the noun, here figuratively 'maddened/intoxicated' describing the elephants. The whole phrase indicates the forces he deployed: 'with warriors and frenzied elephants.'
segments.s101-g23: وزان is a compound prepositional/adverbial phrase meaning 'from that (time/place)'. روی here means 'side/direction' (i.e. 'from that direction/turn'). رستم is the subject. دلیر and گزین are postnominal adjectives: دلیر 'brave' and گزین an archaic adjective/participle 'chosen/elite' (گزین here describes Rostam as 'the chosen/selected one').
segments.s101-g24: بپیمود is a past (perfective) verbal form meaning 'he traversed/covered' (ب- prefix + پیمود). زی is an archaic preposition meaning 'of/from/under' here linking the action to the sovereign: 'under/for the king of Iran' or 'in the realm of the king of Iran.' شاه ایران‌زمین is the genitival phrase 'the king of the Iranian land.' The clause thus says Rostam traversed/acted in/under the authority or domain of the Iranian king.
segments.s101-g9: بگفتند is a past, active verb meaning 'they said/told' and appears at the clause start (poetic word order). وی را is the object marked with را ('to him'); همه بیش و کم is an idiomatic postverbal group meaning 'everyone, great and small' (همه = everyone, بیش و کم = 'greater and lesser' / 'high and low'). The whole segment reads: 'they told him — everyone, high and low.'
segments.s101-g10: سپهبد شد uses شد as a copular/result verb 'became' (subject 'سپهبد' follows the verb, a common poetic order). از کار ایشان is a causal/prepositional phrase 'because of their deed/act' (از = 'because of' here) and دژم is the resultant predicate adjective 'sullen/grim' describing the spahbed. Literally: 'The spahbed became sullen because of their deed.'
segments.s101-g11: بفرمود is an honorific/perfective verb meaning 'he ordered/commanded'. تا here introduces the content/result clause ('that/so that'); نزد او is a locative phrase 'to his presence'; شد is used narratively ('came to be' / 'arrived') and is followed by قلّون (an obscure word/name). The whole line functions as 'He ordered that Qalun be brought before him.'
segments.s101-g12: ز ترکان is a genitive/relational phrase 'of/from the Turks' modifying what follows or the person named; دلیری is a noun used attributively 'bravery/valour'; گوی here is a substantive/participial use ('speaker' / 'one who speaks' or an epithet formed from گفتن) rather than the meaning 'sphere'; پرفسون is an obscure/rare word (likely an epithet or name). Grammatically this is a string of modifiers attached to the previously introduced person.
segments.s101-g13: بدو is a dative/adverbial 'to him'; گفت here functions as the reporting verb 'he said' (poetic diction), followed immediately by an imperative بگزین 'choose'. ز لشکر is a partitive phrase 'from the army' and سوار is the noun 'horseman' — together: 'Choose a horseman from the army.' The imperative bگزین governs the following prepositional phrase.
segments.s101-g14: وز here functions as an archaic coordinating/introductory particle linking the next instruction ('and/from here'); ایدر is the adverb 'here'; برو is the imperative 'go'; تا marks the goal/destination 'to/until'; در کوهسار is the locative phrase 'into the mountain‑region.' Together: 'and from here go to the mountains.'
segments.s101-g15: Three adjectives are conjoined with و: دلیر, خردمند, هشیار ('brave, wise, alert'), followed by the imperative باش 'be'. This is a standard vocative/command structure: list of qualities + باش commanding the addressee to possess them.
segments.s101-g16: به پاس اندرون is a locative/purpose phrase 'for the guard of the inner quarters' (به + پاس = 'for the guard'), نیز 'also/likewise' adds emphasis, and بیدار باش is the repeated imperative 'be awake/alert.' The clause reinforces vigilance inside the palace/inner area.
segments.s101-g113: شهنشه (the title) is the subject (the king of kings). چنین is an adverb meaning 'thus/so' modifying گفت. گفت is the past tense verb 'said' (classical Persian past of گفتن) and is followed by با + noun to mark the addressee: با پهلوان = 'to/with the hero'. Word order is Subject + adverb + verb + prepositional phrase for the indirect object.
segments.s101-g114: که introduces the content clause (that / introducing reported speech). خوابی is the noun 'a dream' (accusative object of بدیدم). بدیدم is a past-tense first-person verb 'I saw/dreamt' (here idiomatically 'I dreamed'). به + روشن‌روان is an adverbial/phrase qualifying the manner — literally 'with/to a bright‑soul' meaning 'in a clear-souled frame of mind.'
segments.s101-g115: که again continues the reported clause. از سوی ایران is a prepositional phrase 'from the direction/side of Iran' (از = from, سوی = side/direction, ایران = Iran). دو باز سپید is a noun phrase 'two white hawks' (عدد + noun + adjective, adjective follows the noun in Persian poetry).
segments.s101-g116: یکی is 'one' (indefinite/partitive). تاج رخشان is a compound noun phrase 'a radiant crown' (noun + adjective). به کردار شید is a prepositional/qualifying phrase: به here marks manner/likeness ('in the manner of' / 'as'), کردار ('demeanour, guise') gives the sense 'in the guise/form', and شید is an epithet (here 'beloved' / 'love‑struck') describing that guise.
segments.s101-g117: خرامان و نازان are two adjectival/adverbial participial descriptions ('strutting/proudly' and 'coquettish/coyly') linked by و. شدندی is a past plural verb form 'they became / they were' (classical plural past), and برم here means 'toward me / upon me' (بر + enclitic pronoun 'م' = 'on/at me' or 'toward me'), so the whole segment says they became/appeared strutting and coy toward me.
segments.s101-g118: نهادندی is a past plural verb 'they placed/put' (نهادن). آن تاج را is the definite direct object ('that crown' with object marker را). بر سرم is the prepositional phrase 'upon my head' (بر = on/upon; سرم = 'my head' with possessive suffix). The clause thus reports the hawks placing the crown onto the narrator's head.
segments.s101-g119: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بیدار گشتم is a compound: بیدار (awake) + گشتم (I became / I turned) — together 'I awoke / I became awake.' شدم پرامید follows as a further verbal phrase 'I became hopeful' (شدم = became, پرامید = hopeful), a common classical doubling to stress the change of state.
segments.s101-g120: ازان is a contraction of از + آن 'from that / because of that'. تاج رخشان و باز سپید lists the causes/objects of hope: 'that radiant crown and the white hawk' (tadj + adjective; باز سپید = white hawk). The preposition از indicates source or cause here ('made hopeful by/through').
segments.s101-g81: بگویش is the imperative/commandal form 'tell him' (بگو + -ش 'to him'); که introduces a content clause 'that'. گردان ترا را is an object phrase in which گردان 'troop' takes the 2nd-person possessive suffix ت (your) plus the object marker را, and خواستند is past tense 'they wanted/summoned' placed at the end (typical Persian verb-final order in poetry).
segments.s101-g82: به شادی means 'for/into a joy/celebration' (به + noun), جهانی modifies شادی as 'worldwide/universal', and بیاراستند is a past plural verb 'they adorned/arrayed' (bia-rāstand) agreeing with the implied agents who prepared the festivity.
segments.s101-g83: نشان is the noun 'sign/token' placed before the verb (object-first ordering). ار is the conditional particle 'if'; توانی and دانی are present/subjunctive-second-person forms 'you can' and 'you know' (protasis of the condition). مرا (here split as م + را in the provided segmentation) is the object 'to me' attached to نشان, so the whole phrase means 'a sign to me, if you can and know (how).'
segments.s101-g84: دهی is the (subjunctive/optative) form 'you give' (used after the conditional), و links the two imperatives/requests, به شاهی is the goal 'to kingship' and رسانی is the present/subjunctive 'you bring/let arrive'. ورا is the clitic 'to him/there' (i.e. 'bring him to kingship').
segments.s101-g85: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' or 'at / because of' marking cause ('at Rostam's words'). گفتار رستم is a genitive 'Rostam's speech'; دلیر جوان is the noun phrase 'the brave young man' (adjectives following the noun in Persian poetic word order).
segments.s101-g86: بخندید is simple past 'he laughed' (3rd person), و connects the clauses, گفتش is 'he said to him' (گفت + -ش 'to him'), and که‌ای پهلوان introduces the ensuing direct speech / vocative: که here begins the quoted clause and ای is the vocative 'O' addressing 'پهلوان' 'champion/hero'.
segments.s101-g87: ز تخم فریدون is a genitival prepositional phrase 'of the seed/stock of Fereydun' (ز = 'from/of' in poetic register). منم is the fused copula + pronoun 'I am' (من + ام contracted), and کیقباد is the proper name 'Kay Qobad' in apposition to the speaker.
segments.s101-g88: پدر بر پدر is an idiomatic stacking 'father upon father' meaning 'from father to father / through the generations'; نام دارم is 'I have a name' or 'I bear a (reputed) name' with دارم present 'I have'; به یاد 'in memory/remembered' qualifies how the name is held or passed on.
segments.s101-g41: بدان is an imperative formed from ب + دان (here b is the verbal prefix be- and دان is 'know'), meaning 'know this'. تا introduces purpose/result ('so that' / 'in order that'). همه is the subject 'all/everyone'. دست شادی is a prepositional/nominal phrase 'hands of joy' (i.e. clapping or joyful hands). بریم is a jussive/subjunctive 1st-person plural ('let us go / let us do'), used here to express 'may we all (do X)' — together meaning 'so that we may all clap our hands in joy.'
segments.s101-g42: به یاد is a fixed prepositional phrase 'in memory of / in remembrance of'. رخ نامور is a noun + adjective ('face/visage' + 'renowned'), functioning as the object of remembrance. می‌خوریم is the present tense (indicative/jussive flavor in poetry) of خوردن; here it means 'we drink/celebrate' or more generally 'we fall to/engage in' (poetic use meaning 'we do/perform the act of celebration in memory of').
segments.s101-g43: This segment names the speaker and reports speech. تهمتن is the speaker's name/epithet. بدیشان is a dative/adverbial 'to them'. چنین گفت باز places the speech: چنین 'thus', گفت (here used as 'said') and باز 'again' ('once more said'). Word order is subject/name → indirect object → manner → verb → adverb ('Tahmten to them thus said again').
segments.s101-g44: که‌ای here introduces direct address (classical vocative equivalent to 'O'); نامداران is plural 'renowned/illustrious ones'; گردن‌فراز is an epithet 'proud/lofty-necked' (i.e. haughty). Together they form the vocative 'O renowned, proud ones.'
segments.s101-g45: مرا is the topicalized/contrasted form 'as for me' (object/accusative used for emphasis). رفت is the past-stem verb 'go' (here in a modal/obligatory sense with باید). باید is the impersonal modal 'it is necessary / I must'. به + البرز کوه is the destination 'to Mount Alborz' (البرز کوه = 'the Alborz mountain'). Word order reflects Persian topicalization: 'As for me, (it) must be that I go to Mount Alborz.'
segments.s101-g46: به کاری introduces the purpose/mission 'to a work/undertaking'. که is the relative/connector 'that/which'. بسیار دارد شکوه literally 'has much splendor' (بسیار 'great/many', دارد 'has', شکوه 'splendor/glory'), so the whole clause modifies 'کاری' — 'a task which has much glory.'
segments.s101-g47: نباید is the impersonal negative modal 'one should not / it must not be'. به بالین 'at the pillow/by the bedside' indicates location. سر و دست ناز is a coordinated phrase 'head and hand in nâz' — ناز here means pampering/affection (i.e. indulgent posture). The segment gives a prohibition against luxuriating at one's pillow.
segments.s101-g48: که begins a causal/explanatory clause ('for/because'), پیشست 'is before/lies ahead' (impersonal/existential), بسیار 'great/much', رنج 'suffering/hardship', دراز 'long' — together 'for before you lies much long suffering.' This explains the reason for the prohibition in the previous segment.
segments.s101-g89: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بشنید is the simple past (3rd sg.) of شنیدن 'he heard'; in classical prose/poetry the verb often precedes the subject, so بشنید رستم = 'when Rostam heard'. فرو برد سر is a compound verbal action: فرو (a directional prefix 'downward') + برد (past stem of بردن) = 'lowered/took down', followed by its object سر 'head'. The subject (Rostam) carries over from بشنید to this verb as well.
segments.s101-g90: به خدمت فرود آمد is a participial/finite clause meaning 'he descended/came down to (render) service': به marks direction/purpose 'to/for', خدمت is the object 'service/attendance', and فرود آمد is the compound past 'came down/descended' (فرود + آمد). از تخت زر is the source phrase 'from the golden throne' (از = from, تخت = throne, زر = gold/ golden). Word order is typical poetic inversion with purpose phrase before the verb.
segments.s101-g91: که‌ای here functions as a vocative/poetic address 'O' (an archaic/poetic particle preceding the addressee); it does not carry its usual conjunction meanings. خسرو here means 'king/sovereign' and خسروان is the plural 'kings', so خسرو خسروان جهان = 'king, king of kings of the world' (a doubled title). Note the classical/poetic stacking of titles with genitive relations implied.
segments.s101-g92: پناه بزرگان و پشت مهان is an epithet in two balanced parts: پناه + بزرگان = 'refuge/protector of the great (men)', where بزرگان is the genitive object; و links the phrases; پشت مهان = 'support/backing of guests' (پشت used metaphorically as 'support' or 'protection'). In classical poetry the ezāfe is often elided, so relations are shown by word order and context.
segments.s101-g93: سر تخت ایران is a genitival phrase 'the head/top (crown) of the throne of Iran' (سر = 'top/summit' rather than anatomical head). به کام تو باد is an optative clause: به کام = 'to the wish/pleasure of', تو = 'your', and باد is the optative particle 'may it be'. The verb/particle باد expresses a wish for the preceding noun phrase.
segments.s101-g94: تن ژنده پیلان is a descriptive noun phrase: تن = 'body', ژنده = 'ragged/rough' used adjectivally, پیلان (plural of پیل) = 'elephants' (large creatures), so 'the ragged/hulking bodies of elephants'. به دام تو باد repeats the optative pattern 'may [they] be in your trap/snare' (به = to/into, دام = trap, تو = your, باد = may). The optative particle applies to this clause too, producing a parallel blessing.
segments.s101-g95: نشست تو بر تخت شاهنشهی: نشست here denotes the act/state of sitting or 'seat' (i.e., your enthronement/reign) with تو as possessor; بر تخت شاهنشهی is the prepositional phrase 'upon the imperial throne'. Classical Persian often treats such action-nouns where a modern English would say 'may you sit' or 'may your reign be'.
segments.s101-g96: همت سرکشی باد و هم فرهی is another optative wish. همت سرکشی باد = 'may [you have] the resolve/ambition for boldness/high-spiritedness' (همت = 'ambition/resolve', سرکشی used as a noun meaning waywardness/defiant boldness) with باد as the optative particle. و هم فرهی adds 'and also nobility/refinement', where هم = 'also' and فرهی stands as a noun/adjective 'noble/cultured'; the verb/blessing (باد) is understood to cover both coordinated elements.
segments.s101-g73: سر تخت ایران بیاراستند is an object phrase followed by a past plural verb. سر تخت (literally “head/top of the throne”) functions as a fixed nominal phrase meaning the royal throne; بیاراستند is the 3rd‑person plural past tense ‘they adorned/arrayed’. The verb appears at the end as is normal in Persian clause structure.
segments.s101-g74: This clause names the subject and the action: بزرگان (the nobles) is the subject, به شاهی is a prepositional phrase indicating goal/purpose (‘to/for kingship’), ورا is the object pronoun ‘him’, and خواستند is the 3rd‑person plural past of خواستن ‘to ask/wish’. The construction literally reads ‘the nobles to kingship him asked’ → ‘the nobles asked him to be king.’
segments.s101-g75: پدرم is ‘my father’ (پدر + possessive clitic -م). The copula (‘was’) is omitted in this poetic line (typical in Persian narrative). آن گزین یلان is an NP ‘that chosen of heroes’ (گزین used adjectivally ‘chosen/selected’ modifying یلان ‘heroes’). سر به سر is a repetitive phrase emphasizing primacy/foremost status (‘head to head’, i.e. foremost among them) modifying گزین یلان.
segments.s101-g76: که introduces a relative clause describing the father. خوانند here is the (habitual/historical) present plural ‘they call’ (from خواندن with the sense ‘to call/name’), او را is the accusative ‘him’, همی is an adverbial particle marking habitual/ongoing action, and زال زر is the name/epithet ‘Zal the Golden’ functioning as what they call him.
segments.s101-g77: مرا گفت is ‘he said to me’ (مرا = me as object; گفت = ‘said’), and رو here is an imperative/shortened form meaning ‘turn/go’ (used to introduce the reported command). تا introduces the extent ‘to/as far as’, followed by the destination به البرز کوه ‘to the Alborz mountain’. The clause frames reported speech: ‘he said to me: go to the Alborz mountain…’.
segments.s101-g78: This is the continuation of the reported command. قباد is the proper name, دلاور is an epithet ‘the brave/valiant’, ببین is the imperative ‘see/meet’, and با گروه ‘with a troop/company’ indicates accompaniment. Word order (name + epithet) is normal in poetry; ببین is the directive to the addressee.
segments.s101-g79: به شاهی برو is an imperative sequence ‘go to the kingship/throne’ (به = to, شاهی = kingship), آفرین کن is a light‑verb construction where آفرین ‘acclamation/praise’ is licensed by کن ‘do’ to mean ‘acclaim’ or ‘cry bravo’, and یکی likely adverbially limits it to ‘once’ or ‘one (single) acclamation’. Imperatives are used to order action for the investiture/celebration.
segments.s101-g80: نباید is an impersonal modal negative ‘one must not/there should not’, که introduces the subordinate clause, سازی here refers to the act/arrangement or the making (ساز often ‘instrument’ but in context ‘the preparing/arranging’), درنگ is ‘delay/pausing’, and اندکی modifies it ‘a little’. Together: ‘There must be no (even the slightest) delay in making/arranging [the celebration].’
segments.s101-g129: کنون (now) + the imperative خیز (rise!) opens a purposive clause introduced by تا (here 'so that / in order that'). سوی ایران شویم uses the subjunctive شویم (from شدن) to express purpose/result: 'that we may come/become toward Iran.' Word order places the imperative then the purpose clause; شویم is subjunctive (optative) rather than a simple past.
segments.s101-g130: به یاری is an adverbial phrase meaning 'with/by way of help' (به as adverbial 'by' + یاری 'help'). The second prepositional phrase به نزد دلیران ('to/into the presence/near the brave') uses به + نزد + noun; the final شویم is again subjunctive 'let us go / let us be' and applies to both preceding prepositional phrases (purpose/intent: to help, to join the brave). Note the duplicated به is a common poetic construction linking two goals.
segments.s101-g131: قباد اندر آمد = 'Qobad came in/entered' (اندر = poetic 'in/within', آمد = past 'came'). چو آتش ز جای is a simile 'like fire from its place' (چو = 'as/like', ز = poetic 'from'), modifying the following action; the simile is placed here for poetic effect.
segments.s101-g132: ببور here is a vigorous verbal form meaning 'cleave/strike through' (poetic/elliptical usage), followed by نبرد 'battle/strife' as its object; اندر آورد پای is an idiom 'to bring the foot into' = 'to step into / plunge into' the fight (آورد is 'bring', پای 'foot', اندر 'in'). Together they describe a rapid, forceful entrance into battle.
segments.s101-g133: کمر برمیان بست رستم = 'Rostam bound his belt/girded his loins' (کمر = belt/waist, برمیان = 'in the midst' here idiomatically marking how he set the girdle). چو باد is a simile 'like the wind' that, though placed here, grammatically modifies the following verb in the next segment (a common poetic enjambment).
segments.s101-g134: بیامد گرازان پس کیقباد = 'boars came after Kay Qobad' (بیامد = 'came', گرازان = 'boars' or 'a host of boars', پس = 'after', کیقباد = the king's name). Word order is verb–subject–postmodifier, giving the sense 'boars came pursuing/after Kay Qobad', with the preceding چو باد supplying the simile 'like the wind.'
segments.s101-g135: شب و روز ('night and day' = continuously) + از تاختن ('from charging/horse‑charge') with the verb نغنوید (negative-present form of غنود/غنو 'to sleep/abate') means 'they did not stop from charging' or 'they did not cease their charge.' The initial negative n- prefix yields the sense 'did not slumber/abate,' i.e., continuous activity.
segments.s101-g136: چنین ('thus/so') + تا ('until') introduces the limit: 'thus until ... reached.' به نزد طلایه رسید = 'he/they reached the vanguard' (به = to/toward, نزد = near/presence of, طلایه = vanguard, رسید = past 'reached'). The clause expresses the eventual result of the continuous charge.
segments.s101-g153: Word order is Subject-Verb-Object if you read 'نامدار' (the famed one) as the subject after the verb: 'ستد' (he seized) + 'نیزه' (the spear) + 'از دست او' (from his hand) + 'نامدار' (the famed one, an epithet referring to the actor). 'ستد' is a simple past/action verb. 'از دست او' is a prepositional phrase marking the source/possessor (from his hand).
segments.s101-g154: 'بغرید' is a past/epic present verb meaning 'roared' or 'bellowed'; it is used here for an inanimate/animate effect (the spear or the action making a roaring sound). 'چون' introduces a simile 'چون تندر' = 'like thunder', and 'از کوهسار' (from the mountain/highland) completes the image. The clause is linked to the previous by describing the sound/effect that followed the seizure.
segments.s101-g155: 'بزد' is a past tense verb 'he struck' (here transitive/instrumental: struck with the spear). The conjunction 'و' links two actions; 'برگرفتش' (برگرفت + -ش) is a past tense verb 'he picked it up / raised it' with the enclitic pronoun -ش referring back to the spear. 'ز زین' uses the poetic preposition 'ز' = 'from' and 'زین' = 'saddle', indicating the source from which he lifted the spear.
segments.s101-g156: 'نهاد' here is the past of 'نهادن' = 'placed/put'. The direct object 'آن بن نیزه را' ('that' + 'butt/base of the spear' + object marker 'را') is placed by the verb; 'بر زمین' (= 'on the ground') is the locative phrase. Word order is Verb + Object + Locative, common in classical Persian narrative.
segments.s101-g157: The pattern is Subject ('قلون') + Verb ('گشت', 'became/turned') + simile introduced by 'چون' ('like'). 'مرغ با بابزن' is the image used in the simile ('a bird with a door‑knocker'); this is a compact poetic simile following the verb. 'قلون' here functions as a proper name/term and so is the clause's subject.
segments.s101-g158: 'بدیدند' is the past-tense plural 'they saw'. The object 'لشکر' ('the host/army') is followed by 'همه' ('all/everyone') and the idiom 'تن به تن' ('body-to-body' = hand-to-hand / one-on-one), which describes the state of the army. Word order is Verb + Object + modifiers describing that object.
segments.s101-g159: 'هزیمت شد' is a passive-like verbal phrase meaning 'was routed' or 'a rout occurred'. 'از وی' names the agent/causer ('by him') using the preposition 'از' and the pronoun 'وی'. 'سپاه قلون' (the army/host of Qalun) is the patient/theme being affected. The clause literally reads 'a rout became from him [of] Qalun's army' — i.e., 'he routed Qalun's army.'
segments.s101-g160: 'به یکبارگی' is an adverbial phrase 'in one instant / suddenly'. The phrase 'بخت بد را زبون' is structured as Verb omitted (resultative) + object: 'بخت بد' = 'ill luck / bad fate' (marked by object marker 'را') + 'زبون' = 'abject/humbled'. In classical narrative this compact clause means 'they instantly humbled ill fate' or 'ill fate was made abject in an instant.'
segments.s101-g137: This clause is a compact nominal + predicate line: the name 'قلون' (here a proper name) is followed by the epithet 'دلاور' (brave) and the past verb 'شد' meaning 'became'. 'آگه ز کار' is a prepositional phrase: 'آگه' (aware) governed by 'ز' = 'of/from' and the noun 'کار' = 'affair/deed'. Word order is Name + epithet + verb + complement (typical Persian order where the predicate follows its subject phrase).
segments.s101-g138: This temporal clause begins with the subordinating particle 'چو' = 'when'. 'آتش بیامد' is a simile/metaphor: 'آتش' (fire) with the past verb 'بیامد' (came) describes a rapid motion or ardor. 'سوی کارزار' is a prepositional phrase 'toward the battlefield'. The whole clause modifies the timing/manner of the main clause (i.e. when he rushed like fire to the field).
segments.s101-g139: 'شهنشاه ایران' is the title + proper place-name (the Shahanshah of Iran) as the subject. 'چو' = 'when' introduces a temporal clause; 'زان گونه' = 'that manner/way' (زان = 'that, from that' + گونه = 'manner'), and 'دید' is the past verb 'saw/observed'. The clause literally reads 'the Shahanshah of Iran, when he saw that manner'.
segments.s101-g140: 'برابر' here means 'opposite/against' and functions adverbially. 'همی' is the archaic/progressive particle often used with past verbs in epic poetry (here indicating the action was about to / intended to take place). 'خواست' is the past of 'to want' = 'wished', and 'صف برکشید' is a verbal phrase 'to draw up/raise the battle-line' (صف = battle line, برکشید = drew up/raised). Word order places the modal desire before the infinitival/verb phrase being desired.
segments.s101-g141: 'تهمتن' is the speaker's name/epithet (proper noun). 'بدو' = 'to him' (dative), and 'گفت' is the reporting verb 'said' (past). 'کای شهریار' is a vocative: 'کای' = 'O' addressing 'شهریار' = 'king'. The clause structure is Speaker + indirect object + said + vocative.',
segments.s101-g142: This is the content of Tahmatan's speech. 'ت' + 'را' together form the pronoun 'ترا' (you) with 'را' marking it as the object/target of the remark. 'رزم جستن' is an infinitival verbal noun phrase: 'رزم' = battle, 'جستن' = to seek/attempt — literally 'to seek battle'. 'نیاید' is an impersonal/3rd-person present-past negative form meaning 'does not become / is not fitting' and 'بکار' (بِ کار) = 'to the task' / 'fit/useful' so together 'نیاید بکار' = 'is not fitting/it does not suit'. Word order: object + verb phrase + negative predicate that predicates suitability.
segments.s101-g143: This is a simple coordination: 'من و رخش و کوپال و برگستوان' lists 'I' plus three companions/items. The repeated 'و' = 'and' simply strings the coordinated nouns together. Each noun acts as a conjunct in the subject group; note some listed lemmas are proper names (Rakhsh, Kupāl).
segments.s101-g144: 'همانا' is an emphatic adverb meaning 'indeed/verily'. 'ندارند' is the 3rd-person plural present/past-contrasted form of 'داشتن' used here as 'they do not have'. 'با من' = 'with me' (comparing or 'in relation to me'), and 'توان' is a noun meaning 'strength/ability'. Together the clause says 'indeed they do not have strength compared to me' (i.e. they lack my power).
segments.s101-g49: This is a vocative phrase made of epithets addressed to the ruler. سر here is an honorific 'chief/head' (addressing rank), تخت is 'throne', ایران names the realm, آبی (آبی) is a descriptive adjective 'blue' attached after the noun as in older Persian poetic order, and شهریار is 'king/lord'. The whole string lists titles and is not a finite clause.
segments.s101-g50: مرا باده خوردن is a nominalized verb construction meaning 'my drinking wine' or better 'to drink wine (for me)'; here مرا is the object/benefactive 'for me' (classical 'مرا' = 'me'), باده خوردن is the verbal noun 'drinking wine'. نیاید is an impersonal negative/it-is-not-fitting construction ('does not befit / is not appropriate'), and به کار is an idiomatic complement meaning 'to be of use / appropriate'. Together: 'For me, wine‑drinking is not fitting.'
segments.s101-g51: نشانی دهیدم is an imperative/requestal clause: نشانی 'a sign/directions' + دهیدم = دهید (give, 2pl imperative) + enclitic م ('me') → 'give me' or 'show me'. سوی 'toward' marks the destination, and کیقباد names the addressee/target. The literal sense: 'Give me directions toward Kay Qobad.'
segments.s101-g52: This is a follow-up interrogative/relative: کسی 'someone/anyone', کز (که + از contracted) introduces the relative 'who/from whom/that', شما 'you (pl.)', دارد 'has/holds' used idiomatically with به یاد to mean 'remembers', او 'him', را the definite object marker, به یاد the compound meaning 'in memory / remembers'. Together: 'anyone among you who remembers him?'.
segments.s101-g53: سر آن here functions as a temporal/transitional phrase 'upon that / then' (classical poetic usage). آن دلیران is 'those brave men' (آن + plural adjective/collective), زبان برگشاد is a fixed expression 'opened his tongue' = 'spoke up'. Word order is typical old Persian poetry: topic (سر آن دلیران) then verb phrase.
segments.s101-g54: که introduces the content clause ('that/which'), دارم is first person present 'I have' (here reported speech), نشانی 'a sign/token', من emphasises the possessor 'my', از 'from', کیقباد names the source. The clause reports the content of what was said: 'that I have my sign from Kay Qobad.'
segments.s101-g55: گر introduces a conditional 'if'. آیی is the present/jussive 2nd-person form 'you come' and is placed before فرود (an adverbial/nominal 'down/descent') — the phrase means 'if you come down'. و links to خوری, a parallel 2nd-person present/jussive 'you eat', so 'and eat our bread.' نان ما is the direct object 'our bread.'
segments.s101-g56: بیفروزی is a 2nd-person/future-tinged verb meaning 'you will brighten/glorify/bring joy' (bi- prefix + root فروخت/فروز? here classical 'بیفروزی' = 'cause to shine/cheer'). از روی خود is a prepositional phrase 'from your face/presence' (روی = face/sight, خود = yourself/your own), and جان ما 'our life(s)/souls' is the object. Together the clause says that by appearing (showing your face) you will cheer/revive our lives.
segments.s101-g105: چون is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing the clause; the verb رسید (past) takes سخن as subject and the prepositional phrase به گوش سپهبد ('to the ear of the spahbed') expresses who heard it. The idiom به گوش رسید means 'was heard by' or 'reached the ear of'.
segments.s101-g106: ز شادی marks cause ('from/because of joy'); دل اندر = 'the heart within' (اندر = 'in/within'). برش here is the cliticized form (بر + ش) meaning 'in/for/on him' (not listed among the supplied senses). برطپید is a past tense compound meaning 'began to throb/beat' (بر- prefixed verb), so the whole phrase says his inner heart beat with joy.
segments.s101-g107: بیازید is an archaic/perhaps poetic perfective verb meaning something like 'produced/poured/uncorked' (the exact sense is irregular here and not in the supplied senses). جامی لبالب نبید is a noun phrase: جامی 'a cup' modified by لبالب 'to the brim' and نبید 'wine' (classical word order places the wine noun after جام). The ezāfe is omitted poetically.
segments.s101-g108: بیاد تهمتن = 'in memory/honour of Tahmatan' (بیاد = 'in memory of'). به دم is an adverbial phrase 'in one breath/at a moment' (به used adverbially), and درکشید (here 'drew in' or 'pulled in') describes taking the drink quickly — a conventional poetic way to say he drank it at once.
segments.s101-g109: This string lists the drinkers then the drink: 'تهمتن همیدون' are names (Tahmatan and Hamidun), followed by 'یکی جام می' where یکی = 'one/a single' (here 'a'), جام = 'cup' and می = 'wine' — classical word order places the substance (می) after جام to mean 'a cup of wine.'
segments.s101-g110: بخورد is a past, perfective form meaning 'he drank' (here used of the preceding subject); آفرین کرد is a fixed phrase 'he cried/bravo!' or 'he expressed praise' (کرد is the past of 'to do'); بر جان is the prepositional phrase 'upon the life/soul (of...)' and کی is ambiguous in isolation (lemma lists 'who'/'when') but here functions as the identifying word attached to جان — contextually it points to 'Kay/the king' or 'whose life' (the supplied senses do not include the proper-name reading).
segments.s101-g111: برآمد (past) 'arose/issued forth' with خروش as the subject 'a cry/roar arose'. از دل = 'from the heart' and the collocation زیر و بم is an idiom meaning 'depths and heights' or 'every part' of the heart; بم does not match the supplied sense list and is therefore marked absent in the senses provided.
segments.s101-g112: فراوان شده is a resultative/perfect construction 'has become abundant' with شادی as the subject that has become abundant; اندوه کم is a succinct clause 'sorrow (is) little' (کم used predicatively/adverbially). Word order is inverted compared with modern colloquial Persian but typical in poetry.
segments.s101-g121: بیاراستم is a past first-person verb (I adorned / I set up), an archaic/poetic preterite form placed before its object (inversion common in poetry). مجلسی شاهوار is the noun مجلسی (“assembly”) with the adjective شاهوار (“regal, lofty”) following it; together they form the direct object of the verb.
segments.s101-g122: برین سان is the fused, poetic form of بر این سان (“in/on this manner”); بر = 'on/upon', ین = orthographic variant of این, سان = 'manner/so'. که introduces the result/clause (‘so that’ / ‘as’). بینی is 2nd-person singular present (“you see”) used here as a simple declarative/presentive. بدین is a contracted classical locative/phrase (equivalent to به این ‘in/at this’); مرغزار is the location noun ‘meadow’ that the clause points to.
segments.s101-g123: The clause places the proper name تهمتن (Tahmatan) in subject position; مرا is the first‑person object (‘to me’ / ‘me’) split in the word list, and را is the object marker following it. شد is past (“became/appeared”); چو is the simile marker here meaning ‘like/as’; باز here is the noun ‘hawk/falcon’ and سپید is the adjective ‘white’ modifying باز.
segments.s101-g124: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; تاج بزرگان is a genitival phrase ‘the crown of the great ones/elders’. رسیدم is first‑person past of رسیدن (here: 'reached me' / 'I received (news)') and نوید is the object ‘good news/omen’ that has arrived.
segments.s101-g125: چو here functions temporally as 'when'. بشنید is past third‑person singular 'he heard'. از خواب شاه is a prepositional phrase 'from (the) sleep/dream of the king' (خواب شاه = 'the king's dream'), indicating the source of what was heard.
segments.s101-g126: This is a short coordinated noun phrase introduced by ز ... و ز ... (‘from/of the … and from/of the …’) listing the items heard: باز = 'hawk/falcon', و = 'and', تاج = 'crown'. فروزان is an adjective 'radiant/bright' and چو ماه is a simile 'like the moon' modifying فروزان.
segments.s101-g127: چنین 'thus' + گفت normally yields 'thus said'; the word list's lemma for گفت lacks the verbal sense, so that specific sense is flagged below. با شاه means 'to/with the king' (با here marking the addressee), and کنداوران is the agent noun 'interpreter/investigator' who is speaking; word order places the speaker and addressee together in the clause.
segments.s101-g128: نشانست is a copular/existential expression 'it is a sign'. خوابت is خواب + -ت, the possessive suffix -ت = 'your dream' (object/topic). ز پیغمبران = 'from the prophets' with ز as the preposition 'from' and پیغمبران 'prophets' as the source of the sign.
segments.s101-g161: تهمتن is the subject (the hero's epithet). گذشت is the past 3rd‑person of گذشتن and with از + NP means 'passed by/past'. طلایه سوار is a compound/group 'vanguard riders' (طلایه = vanguard, سوار = rider). The phrase literally reads 'Tahmatan passed from the vanguard riders.'
segments.s101-g162: بیامد is a literary past form 'he came'. شتابان is an adverbial adjective 'hastily/with speed' modifying the verb. سوی + noun is a prepositional phrase 'toward(s) the mountain' (سوی = toward, کوهسار = mountain/highland). Word order places the verbal phrase before the directional phrase.
segments.s101-g163: کجا here functions as a locative/interrogative particle introducing 'to what place' (used rhetorically). بد modifies علفزار meaning 'poor/unfavourable meadow' (poetic adjective after noun is common). و آب روان coordinates a second locative element 'and flowing water' (آب روان = running/flowing water).
segments.s101-g164: فرود آمد is a compound meaning 'alighted/descended' (فرود as noun/verb + آمد 'came'). آن جایگه is the locative 'to that place' and پهلوان is the subject 'the hero/champion' placed after the verb (inverted subject–verb order typical in poetry).
segments.s101-g165: چنین 'thus' sets the manner. تا introduces a temporal clause 'until'. شب تیره = 'the dark night' (تیره follows شب in poetic word order). آمد فراز is a poetic sequence where آمد 'came' takes فراز ('on high/over') to mean 'came over' or 'fell (upon)'.
segments.s101-g166: تهمتن is the subject. همی is an archaic/progressive particle indicating continued or habitual action ('kept/was'), combined with کرد (past of کردن) to mean 'kept doing'. هرگونه ساز is the object 'every kind of device/arrangement' (ساز here = means/arrangement rather than musical instrument).
segments.s101-g167: از introduces a partitive/source 'from' (part of the trappings). آرایش جامه پهلوی is a genitive/nominal phrase 'the adornment/array of the side‑garment' (جامه = garment, پهلوی = 'side' or 'side‑robe'), with the modifiers following in poetic order.
segments.s101-g168: همان = 'that very' (emphatic demonstrative) modifies تاج 'crown'. و هم = 'and also'. باره here means 'load/burden' in a figurative/poetic sense often used for a cloak or royal accoutrement, modified by خسروی 'royal'. The phrase lists items of regalia taken from the garment's adornment.
segments.s101-g97: درودی is a noun 'a greeting' placed before the verb for emphasis; رسانم is 1st person singular (‑am) present/subjunctive form of رساندن meaning 'I send / I convey'. The prepositional phrase به شاه جهان ('to the king of the world') marks the indirect recipient (به + noun).
segments.s101-g98: ز here is the poetic ablative/agent marker 'by / from'. گزین is used adjectivally/participially ('chosen' / 'select') and is followed by its agent ز زال 'chosen by Zāl'. آن is the demonstrative 'that/the', and یل پهلوان are two apposed nouns 'hero, champion' (yll + pehlavān) describing the same person.
segments.s101-g99: اگر introduces a conditional clause ('if'). شاه فرمان دهد uses دهد (3rd sg. present/subjunctive) in a protasis to mean 'should/the king gives (an order)'. بنده را (lit. 'the servant' + را) functions as the indirect object/dative — a humble self-reference meaning 'to your servant / to me'.
segments.s101-g100: که introduces the content/result clause ('that / so that'). بگشایم is 1st person singular present/subjunctive of گشودن with the prefix ب‑, here 'that I may open/release'. از بند is a prepositional phrase 'from the fetter(s)', and گوینده را ('the speaker' with را) is the definite object being released.
segments.s101-g101: قباد is the proper name (subject) followed by the epithet دلاور ('the brave/valiant'). برآمد is the simple past 'rose / stood up'. ز جای is the ablative phrase 'from (his) place/seat' indicating motion away from where he sat.
segments.s101-g102: ز here functions causally 'because of / from' introducing the cause (گفتار رستم). The following sequence دل و هوش و رای lists three faculties 'heart, intellect, and counsel/decision' that are affected; the verb is omitted (elliptical) but the sense is 'were moved / were stirred by Rostam's speech.'
segments.s101-g103: تهمتن is an epithet/name (subject). همانگه is an adverb 'at that instant / immediately'. زبان برگشاد is an idiomatic verb phrase: زبان ('tongue/speech') plus برگشاد (past of برگشودن 'opened'), literally 'opened his tongue' = 'began to speak'.
segments.s101-g104: پیام ('a message') precedes the beneficiary/indirect object سپهدار ایران ('to the commander of Iran') and the verb بداد appears last. بداد here is an archaic/poetic past form meaning 'gave / delivered' (ب + داد). Note: بداد is an older literary verbal form rather than the nominal sense 'oppression'.
segments.s101-g169: چو introduces a temporal subordinate clause ('when'); شب تیره شد is a complete clause ('the night became dark') with شب as subject and شد (past of شدن) as the predicate. پهلو پیش‌بین is a nominal phrase that follows the clause and functions as the subject/agent of the next verb — literally 'the foreseer at the side' (پهلو here = at the side; پیش‌بین = seer).
segments.s101-g170: برآراست is a past transitive verb meaning 'arranged/adorned' (3rd-person singular/past in classical register) whose subject is the preceding 'پهلو پیش‌بین'. باشاه ایران‌زمین is a prepositional/oblique phrase ('with/for the king of the Iranian realm') giving the beneficiary or location; باشاه here means 'with the king' or 'before the king'.
segments.s101-g171: This is a past action: نزدیک زال (Zal near) is the object/goal phrase and آوریدش is the past verb 'they brought him' (آورد + enclitic -ش). The initial به marks the relation ('to/bring to'); the final به شب is an adverbial time phrase ('at night'), placed after the verb as is common in Persian.
segments.s101-g172: به آمد شدن is a nominalized/time clause meaning 'on/when the coming (arrival) occurred' (به + آمد + شدن). هیچ negates the following verb for emphasis ('not at all'/'not one'), نگشاد is the negative past form meaning 'did not open' (ن‑ + گشاد), and لب is the object 'lip(s)'. Word order places the negator and verb before the object.
segments.s101-g173: نشستند is the past plural 'they sat' (here meaning 'they convened'/'they remained seated') and یک هفته expresses duration ('for one week'). با رای زن is an instrumental/agentive phrase ('with the counsel/advice of the woman') that modifies or motivates the action.
segments.s101-g174: شدند is past plural of شدن ('they became' / 'they were made'); اندران ('among them') is a locative/prepositional adverb, موبدان is the plural subject/object 'the mobeds (Zoroastrian priests)', and انجمن is the noun 'assembly' — together meaning 'an assembly of mobeds was formed among them' (classical Persian often permits this inverted order).
segments.s101-g175: بیاراست is a past/transitive verb 'adorned' or 'decorated'; the preceding element بهشتم functions as the place/object ('the palace/their behesht') that was adorned. پس marks sequence ('then'), and تخت عاج is a noun phrase 'an ivory throne' (تخت = throne; عاج = ivory) introduced as the next item prepared.
segments.s101-g176: برآویختند is past plural 'they raised' or 'they hung upon'; the following prepositional cluster از بر عاج تاج literally strings the material and placement: از (of/from), بر (upon/on), عاج (ivory) and تاج (crown) — i.e. 'they hung/placed a crown of ivory upon (it)'. Persian often stacks small prepositions like از بر to indicate 'of/on'.
segments.s101-g145: بگفت این و — 'بگفت' is a past simple verb 'he said'; 'این' is the demonstrative 'this'; 'و' links clauses ('and' / 'then'). 'از جای برکرد رخش' is a classical word order where the verb 'برکرد' (= 'rose/raised') follows the prepositional phrase 'از جای' ('from his place') and its subject 'رخش' (the proper name of the steed) appears at the end; literally 'from his place Rakhsh rose.'
segments.s101-g146: به زخمی سواری همی کرد پخش — 'همی کرد' is the habitual/continuous past auxiliary + verb pattern (classical use of همی marking continuing action) meaning 'he was doing / he did (continually)'; 'به' here is instrumental/adverbial ('by/with' or 'in the state of'); 'زخمی سواری' describes the agent/state ('wounded, riding' — i.e. riding while wounded); 'پخش کرد' is a verb phrase 'caused scattering' (пخش = 'scatter'), so the whole clause means roughly 'wounded, he rode and caused the horsemen to scatter.'
segments.s101-g147: قلون دید دیوی بجسته ز بند — 'دید' is the simple past 'he saw'; 'قلون' is a proper name here (subject). 'دیو بجسته' contains the poetic past/participle 'بجسته' meaning 'having leapt/sprung' (classical diction), and 'ز بند' is a prepositional phrase 'out of/from the bond(s)'. Word order places the participial phrase after the noun it modifies: 'he saw a demon having leapt from its bonds.'
segments.s101-g148: به دست اندرون گرز و برزین کمند — this is an instrumental/possession phrase: 'به دست اندرون' = 'in (his) inner/near hand' (i.e. held in his hand); 'گرز' = 'mace/club' and 'و برزین کمند' adds 'and Brzin's lasso'. The preposition 'به' is used here adverbially/instrumentally ('with / in the hand').
segments.s101-g149: برو حمله آورد مانند باد — 'برو' is an intransitive/imperative-like verbal particle meaning 'on he went / he rushed'; 'حمله آورد' is a verbal compound 'brought an attack' = 'launched an assault'; 'مانند باد' is a simile 'like the wind'. Put together: 'he rushed to the attack like the wind.'
segments.s101-g150: بزد نیزه و بند جوشن گشاد — 'بزد' is past 'he struck/struck at'; 'نیزه' is the instrument/object 'the spear'; 'و' links two objects; 'بند جوشن' = 'the link/fastening of the cuirass/mail' (compound noun), and 'گشاد' is a past/adjectival result 'became open / gaped / was opened.' So the clause says he struck with the spear and opened/broke the links of the armor.
segments.s101-g151: تهمتن بزد دست و نیزه گرفت — 'تهمتن' is the hero's name (Tahmātan) and the clause has two coordinated actions: 'بزد' (he struck) and 'دست و نیزه گرفت' (he took/grasped the hand and the spear). Classical Persian often chains verbs like this without repeating the subject, so both verbs have the same subject 'Tahmatan.'
segments.s101-g152: قلون از دلیریش مانده شگفت — 'از دلیریش' is a causal phrase 'because of/from his bravery' (دلیری + possessive -ش = 'his bravery'); 'مانده' is a perfect/resultative participle meaning 'left/was left' (i.e. remained in a state), and 'شگفت' is an adjectival remark 'astonished / struck with wonder' (classical usage). Together: 'Qalun was left amazed at his bravery.'

sentences.s101-s1: From the Turks came a large vanguard, arriving in ill plight; among them arrived a hero, refuge of the battle-line.
sentences.s101-s2: He leapt into battle with the famed warriors; one man, bull-bodied, brandished a mace in his hand.

sentences.s101-s4: They turned their heads toward Afrasiab; all had hearts full of blood and eyes full of water (tears).

sentences.s101-s34: He handed the other cup into Rostam's hand and said to him, 'O renowned one and ...',

sentences.s101-s36: Rostam said to him that he had brought a message concerning the champion to the fair‑souled one.

sentences.s101-s18: A stately, kingly assembly, unadorned, like paradise in colour and ornament.
sentences.s101-s19: When they saw the champion on the road, they received him from that shady place.
sentences.s101-s20: For we are hosts, and (you are) our guest — come down here at our command.

sentences.s101-s31: They came (up) to the river's brink; they sat down beneath that shade.
sentences.s101-s32: A young man rose from his throne; Rostam took him by the hand.
sentences.s101-s13: One roadside milestone on the road up to Mount Alborz; one place he saw — a youthful splendour.
sentences.s101-s14: Many trees and flowing water; a sitting-place of young people.
sentences.s101-s15: A couch had been placed near the water; upon it pure musk and rosewater had been poured.
sentences.s101-s16: A youth, in the guise of the shining moon, sat upon that couch in the shady spot.
sentences.s101-s9: For the Iranians are a people who suddenly rush upon the vanguard.
sentences.s101-s10: He came forth from the presence of Khosrow Qalun and led the people toward the inner quarters.
sentences.s101-s11: He stationed himself on the road opposite the notable men, with warriors and maddened elephants.
sentences.s101-s12: From that point Rostam, brave and chosen, crossed the realm under the king of Iran.
sentences.s101-s5: They all, great and small, told him; the spahbed grew sullen at their deed.
sentences.s101-s6: He commanded that Qalun be brought before him; (he was) a speaker/figure from the Turks noted for bravery (پرفسون is an obscure epithet).
sentences.s101-s7: He said to him, 'Choose a horseman from the army, and from here go to the mountain‑region.'
sentences.s101-s8: Be brave, wise, and alert; keep guard over the inner quarters as well — be awake.
sentences.s101-s57: The king of kings thus said to the hero, 'I dreamed a dream in a clear-souled frame of mind.'
sentences.s101-s58: That from the direction of Iran two white hawks came, one bearing a radiant crown in the guise of a beloved.
sentences.s101-s59: Strutting and coquettish they approached me, and they set that crown upon my head.
sentences.s101-s60: When I awoke I became hopeful because of that radiant crown and the white hawk.
sentences.s101-s41: Tell him that they have summoned your troop and have dressed it up for a joyous celebration of the world.
sentences.s101-s42: If you can and you know (how), give me a sign and bring him to kingship.
sentences.s101-s43: At Rostam's words the brave young man laughed and said to him, 'O champion...'
sentences.s101-s44: I am Kay Qobad, sprung from the seed of Fereydun; I bear my father's name, handed down from father to father in memory.
sentences.s101-s21: Know this: so that we may all clap our hands in joy, we drink/celebrate in memory of the famed face.
sentences.s101-s22: Tahmten again said to them thus: 'O illustrious, proud ones.'
sentences.s101-s23: I must go to Mount Alborz to a task that has great splendor.
sentences.s101-s24: Do not lie pampered with head on the pillow and hand in caress, for much long suffering lies before you.
sentences.s101-s45: When Rostam heard, he lowered his head and came down from the golden throne to offer his service.
sentences.s101-s46: O king, king of kings of the world, refuge of the great and support of guests,
sentences.s101-s47: May the crown of Iran's throne be at your desire, may the hulking bodies of elephants fall into your snare.
sentences.s101-s48: May your seat be upon the imperial throne; may bold resolve and also nobility be yours.
sentences.s101-s37: The nobles adorned the throne of Iran and asked that he assume the kingship.
sentences.s101-s38: My father was that chosen of champions, foremost among them, whom they call Zal the Golden.
sentences.s101-s39: He said to me, “Go to the Alborz mountain; see Qobad the brave with his troop.”
sentences.s101-s40: Go to the kingship and give acclaim once; there should be no delay, not even a little, in the preparations.

sentences.s101-s79: Qalun turned like a bird with a door‑knocker; they saw the host all hand-to-hand.
sentences.s101-s80: Qalun's host was routed by him; in a single instant ill fate was made abject.

sentences.s101-s72: Indeed, neither I nor Rakhsh nor Kupāl nor (my) companion have the strength to match me.

sentences.s101-s26: Show me the way to Kay Qobad — is there anyone among you who remembers him?

sentences.s101-s56: A shout arose from the heart's depths and heights; joy became abundant and sorrow small.
sentences.s101-s61: I set up a regal assembly here in the meadow, as you see.

sentences.s101-s81: Tahmatan rode past the vanguard riders; he came hastily toward the mountain.

sentences.s101-s83: Thus, until the dark night fell, Tahmatan kept making every kind of preparation.

sentences.s101-s50: If the king issues the order to your servant, that I may free the speaker from his bonds.
sentences.s101-s51: Qobād the valiant rose from his place, for Rostam's words had stirred heart, intellect, and counsel.
sentences.s101-s52: Tahmten immediately opened his mouth and delivered his message to the commander of Iran.
